coredata.dc:title: A photocatalysis-self-Fenton system based on NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites at neutral pH and low amount of Fe2+ for the effective degradation of antibiotics

Liu, Yuanhua
Huang, Haiming

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis-self-Fenton combining photocatalytic production of H2O2 with Fenton reaction has been a hotspot, but the pH limitation and iron sludge production problems remain unsolved. Herein, we proposed a self-fenton system based on N-doped carbon dots modified ZnIn2S4 (NCDs@ZnIn2S4) composites that exhibits effective degradation of antibiotics under neutral pH using low amounts of Fe2+. The decoration of ZnIn2S4 with NCDs significantly increased the surface area, visible light absorption, charge transfer ability and oxygen adsorption ability. NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites exhibited a high H2O2 production rate (1528 μM g−1•h−1) under visible light, which was 1.9 and 5.3 times higher than ZnIn2S4 and NCDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 system with a low concentration of Fe2+(1 mg/L) could remove over 95% levofloxacin and oxytetracycline within 30 min. Interestingly, the highest degradation efficiency occurred under neutral pH. Quenching experiments and analytical measurements indicated that the high catalytic performance under pH = 7 with low amounts of Fe2+ stemmed from the higher amount of inner-generate H2O2 under neutral pH and easy regeneration of Fe2+ by photoinduced electrons for high •OH yields. Additionally, the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 system exhibited high degradation performance under different water matrix and ultrahigh degradation efficiency towards levofloxacin under real sunlight irradiation. The work shows the prospects of photocatalysis-self-Fenton systems for overcoming the pH limitation and the difficulty of iron sludge separation in the purification of effluents.

head.ce:title: A photocatalysis-self-Fenton system based on NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites at neutral pH and low amount of Fe2+ for the effective degradation of antibiotics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis-self-Fenton combining photocatalytic production of H2O2 with Fenton reaction has been a hotspot, but the pH limitation and iron sludge production problems remain unsolved. Herein, we proposed a self-fenton system based on N-doped carbon dots modified ZnIn2S4 (NCDs@ZnIn2S4) composites that exhibits effective degradation of antibiotics under neutral pH using low amounts of Fe2+. The decoration of ZnIn2S4 with NCDs significantly increased the surface area, visible light absorption, charge transfer ability and oxygen adsorption ability. NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites exhibited a high H2O2 production rate (1528 μM g−1•h−1) under visible light, which was 1.9 and 5.3 times higher than ZnIn2S4 and NCDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 system with a low concentration of Fe2+(1 mg/L) could remove over 95% levofloxacin and oxytetracycline within 30 min. Interestingly, the highest degradation efficiency occurred under neutral pH. Quenching experiments and analytical measurements indicated that the high catalytic performance under pH = 7 with low amounts of Fe2+ stemmed from the higher amount of inner-generate H2O2 under neutral pH and easy regeneration of Fe2+ by photoinduced electrons for high •OH yields. Additionally, the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 system exhibited high degradation performance under different water matrix and ultrahigh degradation efficiency towards levofloxacin under real sunlight irradiation. The work shows the prospects of photocatalysis-self-Fenton systems for overcoming the pH limitation and the difficulty of iron sludge separation in the purification of effluents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The frequently detected antibiotics (β-lactams, quinolones, tetracyclines) in water bodies have become a worldwide concern (Lin et al., 2024; Su et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024a; Zhang et al., 2024). The high toxicity and low biodegradability of antibiotics as well as ARGs pose a threat to the ecosystem and human health. Various catalytic oxidation processes have been investigated to remove the antibiotics from water, including Fenton, photocatalysis, and electrochemical routes. But single approach is difficult to meet the requirements of practical application. For instance, homogeneous Fenton technology suffers from narrow pH adaptation (2–4) and large amounts of iron sludge (Jin et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2019, 2023), and photocatalytic processes are challenged with inherent drawbacks such as limited light absorption and light utilization rate (Riaz et al., 2015). By contrast, combined processes with two or more water treatment methods have been demonstrated to be more efficient for the degradation of organic pollutants (Bellouk et al., 2023; Brillas and Garcia-Segura, 2020; Shi et al., 2024). Among these combined processes, photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction coupling photocatalytic production of H2O2 with Fenton catalytic oxidation has become a hotspot due to no external addition of H2O2, enhanced Fe2+/Fe3+ cycle and high mineralization rate (Shi et al., 2022a; Sun et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022b).

Extensive research has been published to investigate the performance of self-Fenton systems for the oxidation of refractory organics (Jian et al., 2022; Lian et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2022b). Liang et al. used graphite carbon ring-modified C3N4 (Cg-C3N4) as a catalyst for photocatalytic production of H2O2 and the concentration of H2O2 reached 173.81 μM after 120 min (Jian et al., 2022). After adding Fe2+(1 mM), the concentration of •OH reached 112.45 μM after 120 min. Due to the production of a large amount of •OH, 4-chlorophenol was rapidly degraded and mineralized. In another study, g-C3N4 was coupled with Fe2+ to construct a photocatalysis-self-Fenton system, and 2,4-DCP was almost completely degraded at pH = 3 with a rate constant of 0.91 h−1 (Ma et al., 2020a). Moreover, the synergistic effects between h+ and •OH could break the structure of macromolecular polymers and promote the continuous degradation of pollutant molecules (Zhang et al., 2022b). Despite the great advantage of photocatalysis-self-Fenton systems, its application is faced with many challenges: (1) the production rate of H2O2 needs further improvement to promote Fenton reaction; (2) the degradation processes usually operate under acid condition; (3) the generation of iron sludge remains unsolved due to the supplement of large amounts of iron salts.

ZnIn2S4-based composites with high reduction potential and excellent visible light response have been reported as excellent candidates for photocatalytic H2O2 production (Hu et al., 2022; Shao et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a). Additionally, carbon materials (eg.C3N4, carbon black) could act as cocatalysts to further increase H2O2 production. Previously, our group demonstrated that O-CB/ZnIn2S4 composites possessed a high H2O2 production rate of 1985 μM g−1•h−1 under simulated solar light (Li et al., 2023c). Apart from CB, Carbon dots (CDs) with small particle size (2–5 nm) and excellent fluorescence upconversion properties become good candidate to modify ZnIn2S4 composites for H2O2 production under visible light irradiation (Xu et al., 2024). Moreover, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) could further increase visible light absorption and the separation of photogenerated electron/hole pairs of CDs (Wang et al., 2017). Additionally, carbon materials with abundant C-O/C=O groups have shown as excellent cocatalysts enhancing 2 e− ORR (O2+2e−→H2O2 or O2+e−→•O2 −→H2O2) (Kim et al., 2018; Xia et al., 2019). Therefore, it can be inferred that the combination of NCDs with ZnIn2S4 could enhance H2O2 production under visible light. Meanwhile, the high reduction potential of ZnIn2S4 is beneficial to the regeneration of Fe2+, which is expected to reduce the usage of iron salts.

Herein, NCDs modified ZnIn2S4 were ingeniously designed as visible-light responsive photocatalysts (NCDs@ZnIn2S4) for in-situ H2O2 production and subsequently coupled with Fe2+ constructing photocatalysis-self-Fenton systems for the degradation of antibiotics. The crystal phase, morphology, and optoelectronic properties of as-prepared catalysts were characterized. Subsequently, the photocatalytic H2O2 production ability of NCD@ZnIn2S4 composites and the self-fenton degradation performance of Fe2+/NCD@ZnIn2S4 were investigated. The underlying mechanism of excellent photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation performance at near-neutral pH with a low amount of Fe2+ was revealed. Additionally, the degradation byproducts generated during the degradation process were detected and the possible degradation pathway was proposed. Finally, the catalytic performance of the proposed photocatalysis-self-Fenton system under real sunlight was examined.

Indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O, 98%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 99.95%), and thioacetamide (C2H5NS, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (C6H8O7), urea (NH4CNO), FeSO4·7H2O, levofloxacin (LEV, C18H20FN3O4), amoxicillin (AMX, C16H19N3O5S), oxytetracycline (OTC, C22H242O9•2H2O), benzoquinone (BQ, C6H4O2), t-butanol (TBA, C4H10O), and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na, C10H142Na2O8•2H2O), L-histidine (LDH), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) and salicylic acid were analytical grade and purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Synthesis of NCDs and CDs . NCDs was prepared by an adapted solvothermal method (Fengliang et al., 2017). Specifically, citric acid and urea with a mass ratio of 3: 1 were added into 15 mL water, stirred for 5 min, and then transferred to 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave keeping heating at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, NCDs suspension was obtained. Then NCDs powder was collected after freeze-drying. Similarly, CDs suspension was prepared using the citric acid as the only precursor. But it should be noted that a negligible amount of CDs powder is collected after freeze-drying. This is because CDs synthesized using only citric acid is of low yield, and the main components are unreacted citric acid and molecular intermediates (Chen et al., 2019; Vallan and Imahori, 2022).

Synthesis of NCDs@ZnIn
2
S
4
. A certain amount of as-prepared NCDs suspension was first added to 40 mL of water. Subsequently, 2.4 mmol thioacetamide, 0.8 mmol InCl3⋅4H2O, 0.4 mmol ZnCl2, and 6 mL of glycerol were sequentially added into the above dispersion and stirred vigorously for 10 min for full dispersion. Afterward, the mixture was placed and heated in an oil bath at 80 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the resulting samples were washed with water and ethanol three times respectively, and dried in a vacuum overnight. The effects of the dosage of NCDs on the photocatalytic activity were explored using different amounts of NCDs solution (100 mg, 250 mg, 400 mg, and 800 mg). For comparison, CDs@ZnIn2S4 was prepared using a similar procedure except for the addition of the same amount of CDs solution and ZnIn2S4 was prepared without the addition of NCDs.
The details about the characterization methods are illustrated in Text.S1.

Typically, 10 mg samples were put into water (100 mL) and stirred for 30 min under dark conditions for full dispersion. Subsequently, the solution was irradiated under a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfect Light Co., Ltd) with a filter (>420 nm). During the reaction, 1 mL solution was sampled at predestined periods and filtered to remove the powder samples before the analysis. The concentration of H2O2 was determined by iodometry. The detailed analytical method was reported in our previous work (Li et al., 2023c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The degradation of antibiotics by different photocatalytic systems
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of different photocatalytic systems was evaluated via the degradation of antibiotics (LEV, OTC, and AMX) under simulated visible light irradiation powered by a 300 W Xe lamp with a filter (>420 nm). The photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction was conducted as follows: (1) 10 mg of catalysts were dispersed in water solution (100 mL) under dark conditions in a reactor equipped with circulating cooling water and the concentration of antibiotics was 20 mg/L; (2) after reaching adsorption equilibrium, a certain amount of Fe2+ solution was put into the solution and the light was turned on to start the reaction. (3) during the process, 1 mL sample aliquots were extracted to analyze the residual concentration of contaminants. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic experiments were conducted under a similar procedure without the external addition of Fe2+. The analysis of the concentration of antibiotics during the photocatalytic reaction was detailed in Text.S2. Additionally, the reaction intermediates were detected by high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS, waters G2-XS qtof).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The investigation of the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The composition of existing active species and their contribution to the degradation process were evaluated by scavenging experiments and EPR (Text.S4). The steady-state concentrations of •OH and •O2 − were analyzed by salicylic acid and NBT as reactants, respectively (Shi et al., 2022b).

Subsequently, the effects of Fe2+ on the decomposition and the transformation of H2O2→•OH were investigated. Firstly, Fe2+ solution (2 g/L) was prepared. Then, 10 mg NCDs@ZnIn2S4 was stirred in 100 mL water (pH = 7) for 30 min for full dispersion under dark conditions. Then a certain amount of Fe2+ solution (0, 25, 50, 100 μL) was added to the solution, which was subject to visible light irradiation. The concentration of H2O2 was measured after 5, 10, 20, and 30 min of photocatalytic reaction. The concentration of Fe2+ in the solution was 0, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/L using 0, 25, 50, and 100 μL of Fe2+ solution, respectively. It should be noted that these reactions were all carried out under pH = 7. Meanwhile, the steady-state concentration of •OH was monitored before and after the addition of Fe2+. Subsequently, the effects of solution pH (3–7) on the production of •OH and •O2 − was also analyzed.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of NCDs, ZnIn2S4, and NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites synthesized with different amounts of NCDs are shown in Fig. S1. NCDs exhibit a broad hump at around 24°, which is ascribed to amorphous carbon. This is consistent with previous studies (Wang et al., 2017). ZnIn2S4 displays three peaks at 2θ = 21.5°, 27.7°, and 47.3°, which correspond to (006) (102), and (110) planes (Li et al., 2023b). Moreover, there is no obvious difference between the X-ray patterns of ZnIn2S4 and NCDs@ZnIn2S4-1, indicating that the insertion of NCDs does not influence the crystal phases of ZnIn2S4. But with the increasing amount of NCDs, the peaks at 21.5° and 27.7° become broader and the peak intensity at 2θ = 27.7°, and 47.3° declined significantly. These results suggest that the addition of larger amounts of NCDs could decrease the crystallinity of ZnIn2S4. The morphology of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites was investigated by SEM and HRTEM(Fig. 1 ). NCDs@ZnIn2S4 particles show nanoflower-like structure with particle size about 200 nm (Fig. 1b). As observed in Fig. 1c, small carbon dots are supported on the structure of ZnIn2S4 nanosheets. EDS mapping clearly illustrates the homogeneous distribution of elements of C, N, S, Zn, In, and O, indicating the successful incorporation of NCDs with ZnIn2S4(Fig. 1d). Moreover, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 are shown in Fig. S2. NCDs@ZnIn2S4 exhibits ultra-high surface area (303.85 m2/g) and hierarchical pore structure.

The surface elemental composition and the chemical states of NCDs, ZnIn2S4, and NCDs@ ZnIn2S4 composites are characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Fig. S3 shows the full spectrum and C1s, N1s, and O1s spectrums of NCDs. As observed in Fig. S3b, C=C, C-N(C-OH) and C=O groups occupy 73.02%, 11.11% and 15.88% respectively. The deconvolution curves of N1s and O1s peaks further verify the doping of N and O into the carbon framework, respectively, which is consistent with previous work (Ma et al., 2020b). Moreover, the chemical properties of ZnIn2S4 before and after the addition of NCDs are analyzed (Fig. 2 ). For Zn 2p, two peaks are located at 1022.63 eV (Zn2p5/2) and 1045.70 eV (Zn2p3/2) for ZnIn2S4 composites, but the binding energy of Zn2p5/2 and Zn2p3/2 for NCDs@ZnIn2S4 show a negative shift of −0.6 eV. Similarly, the two peaks corresponding to In3d3/2 and In3d1/2 show a 0.7 eV shift to lower binding energy compared with that for ZnIn2S4. Moreover, S peaks of ZnIn2S4 could be deconvoluted into two subpeaks centered at 161.78 eV, and 163.05 eV, representing S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 respectively. After the insertion of NCDs, the binding energies of S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 also show a negative shift. The negative shift indicates a strong interaction between ZnIn2S4 and NCDs, which could promote the separation and transfer of photo-induced carriers during the photocatalytic process (Du et al., 2022).

The optical and electric properties of ZnIn2S4 before and after the NCDs incorporation were explored. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of ZnIn2S4 and NCDs@ZnIn2S4. After the insertion of NCDs, the absorption of visible light especially in the range of 500–800 nm enhances significantly. The band gap of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 is calculated by converted Tauc plots versus photon energy (hν) (Li et al., 2023b). As shown in Fig. S4a, the Eg value of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 is calculated to be 2.65 eV. Meanwhile, Mott-Schottky plots are used to evaluate the conductive band potential (ECB). As shown in Fig. S4b, the plots for NCDs@ZnIn2S4 exhibit a positive slope, suggesting the n-type semiconductor property. For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential is close to the ECB, thus the ECB of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 is calculated to be −0.90 V vs.NHE. Meanwhile, the EVB is calculated to be 1.75 V vs.NHE. Apart from the visible light absorption, the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers is explored by transient photocurrent experiments and steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3b, ZnIn2S4 exhibits obvious fluorescence at around 450 nm, but the intensity declines significantly after the insertion of NCDs, indicating a lower recombination rate of photogenerated holes and electrons. And the photocurrent intensity of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 (∼0.34 μA/cm2) is about 2.4 times higher than pristine ZnIn2S4 (0.14 μA/cm2), indicating higher amount of photogenerated carriers after the insertion of NCDs. To further investigate the charge transfer behavior in NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites, the work function of NCDs and ZnIn2S4 was calculated. The calculation details are shown in Text.S5. Owing to the lower electrostatic potential values of NCDs (4.762 eV) compared with ZnIn2S4 (5.978 eV), photogenerated electrons tend to migrate from ZnIn2S4 to the surface of NCDs (Di et al., 2019), thus promoting the separation of h+/e− pairs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic production of H2O2 and photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation of antibiotics

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the crucial role of inner-generate H2O2 in the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction, the photocatalytic performance of H2O2 production by different samples was first evaluated under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the incorporation of NCDs with ZnIn2S4 dramatically increased H2O2 production rate. The concentration of H2O2 by NCDs@ZnIn2S4 reached 152.8 μM after 60 min, which was 1.9, 3.6, and 5.3 times higher than ZnIn2S4, CDs@ZnIn2S4 and NCDs, respectively. The lower H2O2 rate by CDs@ZnIn2S4 can be attributed to the low content of carbon nanoparticles in CDs solution (section 2.2). Additionally, the large amounts of unreacted citric acid and molecular intermediates adsorbed on the catalysts could have adverse effects on the physiochemical properties (Hinterberger et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2024). Therefore, the optimal photocatalysts for H2O2 production is NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites. Additionally, the inner-generate H2O2 increased from 139.4 μM to 152.8 μM with the weight of NCDs added changing from 100 mg to 250 mg (Fig. 4b). But further increasing the dosage of NCDs hindered the production of H2O2, which might be due to that excessive carbon dots covered the catalytic center (Vogt et al., 2023). Therefore, 250 mg of NCDs suspension solution was chosen to synthesize NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites. Additionally, the effects of solution pH and input current of Xenon light on H2O2 evolution were investigated (Fig. S5a). After 60 min of visible light irradiation, the concentrations of H2O2 were 153.6, 152.8, 62.7, 21.8, and 6.9 μM at pH = 8.5, pH = 7, 5, 4, and 3, respectively. The production rate of H2O2 was the highest under near-neutral pH, which is in line with previous reports (Hou and Wang, 2017; Hu et al., 2020; Song et al., 2019). This is because acid conditions could decrease 2 e− ORR selectivity by generating •OOH and increase the photo-corrosion, thus hindering the production of H2O2(Hou and Wang, 2017; Sharma et al., 2016; Song et al., 2019; Xue et al., 2022). The photo-leaching of different metal ions at different pH was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5b, the dissolution of metal ions was negligible at pH = 7. But the concentration of dissolved Zn2+ and In3+ increased dramatically with the decrease of solution pH, indicating serious photo-corrosion under acid conditions. Therefore, the optimal pH for H2O2 production is pH = 7. Besides, the H2O2 production efficiency enhanced gradually as the photocurrent increased from 16 A to 20 A (Fig. S5c). Because the highest input current of Xenon light in this study was 20 A, 20 A was determined as the optimal input current.

Moreover, the mechanism of photocatalytic evolution of H2O2 using NCDs@ZnIn2S4 as catalysts was explored. As shown in Fig. 4c, a negligible amount of H2O2 was generated under dark and N2, indicating that H2O2 mainly stemmed from oxygen reduction (ORR). ORR process for H2O2 includes direct 2e- ORR (O2→H2O2) and indirect 2e- ORR (O2→•O2 −→H2O2). EPR analysis showed the generation of •O2 − using ZnIn2S4 and NCDs@ZnIn2S4 as photocatalysts (Fig. S6). To elucidate the role of •O2 − in generating H2O2, the steady-state concentration of •O2 − was measured. The lower amounts of •O2 −(106.4 μM) generated by NCDs@ZnIn2S4(116.5 μM) indicate the minor contribution of •O2 − to H2O2 production. Therefore, it can be inferred that H2O2 is mainly produced by the direct oxygen reduction process. According to UV–Vis diffuse absorption spectra and Mott-Schottky results, the CB and VB were −0.90 and + 1.75 V vs.NHE, respectively. Because the CB was lower than E (O2/H2O2, +0.68 V vs. NHE), O2 could react with photogenerated electrons to form H2O2. But the EVB (1.75 V vs.NHE) was more positive than E (H2O/H2O2, 1.76 V vs. NHE), so H2O2 could not be generated from the oxidation of H2O(Fig. 4e).

Additionally, to better understand the enhanced H2O2 production after the incorporation of NCDs, oxygen adsorption/reduction activity and charge transfer properties were analyzed. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves showed that the onset potential of ORR for NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4 was −0.42 V and −0.36 V (vs. Hg/HgO), respectively (Fig. 4f). This indicates the higher oxygen reduction ability of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 catalysts. Additionally, density functional theory (DFT) is conducted to elaborate on interfacial O2 adsorption and activation processes using VSAP. As shown in Table 1 , The adsorption energy of ZnIn2S4 and NCDs@ZnIn2S4 with oxygen molecules are −0.272, and −0.703 V, respectively. The more negative adsorption energy of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 suggests higher O2 adsorption ability. Moreover, a partial electron transfer of 0.43 e− into O2 from NCDs@ZnIn2S4, two times higher than that of ZnIn2S4 (0.19 e−), indicating better ability to activate O2 (Fig. S7). Besides, steady-state photoluminescence and transient photocurrent tests showed that NCDs/ZnIn2S4 heterojunctions exhibited better separation and transfer of photoinduced pairs (Fig. 3b–c), promoting the migration of electrons and subsequent participation in ORR reaction. These results confirmed the higher H2O2 production by NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites.

Owing to the large amounts of H2O2 in situ produced by NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites, a photocatalysis-self-Fenton system of Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 was conducted for the degradation of various antibiotics under pH = 7 with 1 mg/L Fe2+. LEV, AMX, and OTC were chosen as targeted contaminants due to their frequent detection in the environment. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the dark adsorption experiments were first performed before light irradiation. Composites of ZnIn2S4 and NCDs@ZnIn2S4 exhibited 30% and 45% of LEV, respectively. Despite the enhancement of adsorption rate, the degradation process using NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4 as catalysts was slow and the removal rate was close (∼60% in 30 min). Additionally, almost double the amount of H2O2 was produced by NCDs@ZnIn2S4 compared with that by ZnIn2S4, suggesting the negligible effects of H2O2 to the decomposition of LEV. However, after the addition of Fe2+, the degradation of LEV was greatly enhanced. Over 95% LEV was removed by Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4. And the TOC removal achieved 52.36% after 30 min. Additionally, the kinetic constant for Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based process was as high as 0.075 min−1, which was 3.1, 6.2, 5.3 times higher than Fe2+/ZnIn2S4, NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4-based photocatalytic system, respectively. Similarly, the removal rate of AMX in the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based process was the highest. Over 81% AMX was decomposed after the addition of 1 mg/L Fe2+ within 30 min while no more than 40% AMX was removed in other systems (Fig. 5d). Moreover, the removal rate of OTC increased from 81% to 96% OTC after the addition of 1 mg/L Fe2+ under neutral pH and the reaction rate reached 0.114 min−1 (Fig. S8). The degradation performance of the proposed self-fenton system in this work outperformed many studies (Table S1), indicating the excellent catalytic efficiency.

The solution pH is an important parameter for the Fenton process and the traditional homogeneous Fenton process is limited to a narrow pH range (2–4). Therefore, the effects of solution pH (3–8.5) on the degradation of antibiotics were investigated. Interestingly, the degradation rate towards LEV was over 95% within 30 min at near neutral pH (5–7) while the degradation process slowed down under acid conditions and alkalinity (Fig. 5c). Similarly, the degradation of AMX was also favorable under neutral pH. The removal rate of AMX was 81%, 43%, 29%, 12%, 7.3% at pH = 7, pH = 5, pH = 4, pH = 3, and pH = 8.5, respectively. It can be concluded that neutral pH favored the degradation of antibiotics in the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system. The decline of the degradation efficiency with the decrease of solution pH was also found by Shi et al. (2022a), but a deep understanding was neglected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of degradation intermediates and possible degradation pathway
ce:section.ce:para: The organic intermediates produced under NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 photocatalytic systems were analyzed by HPLC-MS/MS. The mass spectra of degradation products were summarized in Tables S2–S3. Generally, more kinds of organic intermediates were produced in the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system, indicating the higher oxidation ability of the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system. LEV is a complex organic compound with a piperazine ring, oxazine, and quinolone ring. Fukui index calculation shows that C6 was mostly likely to be attacked by radicals (De Witte et al., 2009). Meanwhile, the CH3 groups on piperazine and oxazine rings are easy to remove. According to the chemical structure of LEV and organic intermediates, the possible degradation pathway was proposed for the two systems and shown in Fig. 6 . Firstly, for NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based system, the degradation process could be divided into three degradation pathways: (1) the decarboxylation to obtain T1 (319); (2)the demethylation and subsequent decarboxylation to obtain T3 (260); (3) the alcoholization and depiperazinylation to obtain T6 (291) (Meng et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021). Similarly, the degradation of LEV in the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system was divided into three pathways. In pathway I, the methyl groups at the N-piperazine position were removed to obtain P1 (m/z = 347). The second pathway was characterized by the oxidation and elimination of methylene groups on the piperazine moiety, followed by further decarboxylation reactions to obtain P3 (m/z = 278). Then P3 was transformed into P4 (m/z = 131) after the breakage of the oxazine ring and quinolone group. Meanwhile, P5 (m/z = 165) could be obtained via combined reactions of decarboxylation, decarboxylation, and deamination. The third degradation pathway started with the attack on C6 and then oxidation and ring opening to obtain P6 (m/z = 337). The piperazine moiety could fall off from P6 form P7 (m/z = 100). Additionally, the oxidation of methyl groups at the N-piperazine position leads to the generation of P8 (m/z = 311). Ultimately, the continuous oxidation of the benzene ring and de-fluorination leads to the formation of small acid molecules, such as P9 (m/z = 90) (Wang et al., 2018).

Additionally, the toxicity of the LEV and the degradation products were analyzed via cell viability tests (Text.S6). The results were shown in Fig. S9. The higher percentage of the cell viability, the lower toxicity of the substance to cell. As shown in Fig. S9, lower than 40% of cells were survived after the input of LEV, indicating its toxicity to cells. But with the reaction prolongs, more cells remained, indicating the decrease of toxicity. Although the cell viability ratios decreased a little from 10 min to 20 min, the overall toxicity decreased with reaction time. These results indicate the effective reduction of toxicity of antibiotics via Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of photocatalysis-self-Fenton under neutral pH with a low amount of Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photocatalysis-self-Fenton mechanism, the composition of different active species and their contribution to the removal of LEV were first analyzed (Fig. 7 a). The scavenging experiments were conducted by employing EDTA-2Na, BQ, Catalase, TBA, and LHD as the scavengers for h+, •O2 −/•OH, H2O2, •OH and 1O2, respectively (Li et al., 2020, 2023a; Liu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021). The inhibition rate was obtained by the degree of decrease in degradation rate within 30 min photocatalytic reaction. As seen from Fig. 7a, photoinduced h+ contributed little to the degradation of LEV in both systems. The degradation process of NCDs/ZnIn2S4-based system was significantly suppressed after adding BQ and the removal of LEV was nearly unchanged after the addition of TBA, suggesting the crucial role of •O2 − played in the photocatalytic reaction. By contrast, the main reactive oxidation species (ROS) consisted of H2O2, •OH, and •O2 − in Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based system. Because NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4 composites showed nearly the same removal rate towards LEV despite the big difference in H2O2 production, it can be speculated that H2O2 contributed little to the degradation of LEV. Consequently, the inhibition caused by catalase can originate from the hindrance of •OH production. EPR analysis further verified the production of •OH after the addition of Fe2+(Fig. 7b). To identify the amounts of different radicals in different reaction systems, the steady-state concentration of •O2 − and •OH in different systems was measured. As shown in Fig. S10, the concentration of •O2 − declined to some extent after the addition of Fe2+. By contrast, the concentration of •OH increased sharply after the addition of Fe2+, which was in line with the degradation kinetic constants of LEV in different reaction systems. These results suggest the high degradation rate of the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction stems from the increasing ratios of •OH in radicals.

The generation of •OH relies on the activation of inner-generate H2O2 by Fe2+, therefore the amount of Fe2+ is crucial to photocatalysis-self-Fenton performance. The effects of Fe2+ dosage on the decomposition of H2O2 and transformation of H2O2→•OH were investigated (Figs. S11(a–b)). When no Fe2+ was added, the concentration of H2O2 reached 3.62 mg/L after 30 min of illumination. But after the addition of Fe2+, the concentration of H2O2 decreased rapidly. Almost no H2O2 was observed after the addition of Fe2+(0.5–2 mg/L). This is because inner-generate H2O2 could be decomposed by Fe2+ to produce •OH. When the concentration of Fe2+ increased from 0.5 mg/L to 1 mg/L, the concentration of •OH within 30 min increased from 20.03 μM to 40.86 μM. But further increasing to 2 mg/L did not observe the increase of •OH yields, indicating that 1 mg/L of Fe2+ was adequate for the activation of inner-generate H2O2. Meanwhile, the redox behavior of Fe2+ during the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction was monitored. The concentration of Fe2+ was decreased from the initial 1 mg/L to 0.37 mg/L at 10 min and then increased slightly to 0.47 mg/L at 30 min (Fig. S12). This was because Fe2+ reacted with H2O2 and was then oxidized to Fe3+ at the initial stage, and Fe3+ could be reduced by photogenerated electrons for the regeneration of Fe2+(Liu et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b). By contrast, the concentration of Fe3+ was slowly increased to 0.08 mg/L after the reaction of 20 min but exhibited little change with the prolonging of reaction time. Although Fe3+ can be generated from the oxidation of Fe2+, the concentration of Fe3+ in the solution was low. This was due to the easy precipitation of Fe3+ under neutral pH, causing the decline of total iron concentration. To further verify the reduction from Fe3+ to Fe2+, the phenanthroline spectrophotometric method was employed to investigate the regeneration of Fe2+ in the Fe3+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4 system. Fig. S13 shows new peaks at around 510 nm, which corresponds to Fe2+(Smith et al., 2021). The ability to regenerate Fe2+ in the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction is favorable for the reduction of iron salts dosage.

Additionally, the pH dependence of catalytic degradation efficiency was observed (Fig. 5c and f) and the optimal catalytic performance of the proposed photocatalysis-self-Fenton system occurred under neutral pH. Particularly, the degradation rate of AMX under neutral pH was about 7 times higher than that under pH = 3. Based on previous studies, the solution pH could influence the surface charge properties of catalysts, the ionic states of organic molecules, and the composition of reactive oxygen species (Ahmad et al., 2015; Roman et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022). Firstly, the zeta potential of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 under pH (3–8.5) is negative (Fig. S14). Amoxicillin is an amphoteric substance with three pKa values (pKa 1 = 2.67, pKa 2 = 7.11, and pKa 3 = 9.55) (Kaur et al., 2011). In the pH range 3–7, the majority of AMX was amphoteric; at pH > 7.11, it is anionic; and at pH > 9.55, it is an anion with two negative charges. From Figs. 5f and 8%–15% of AMX was adsorbed by the catalysts under pH (3–7) but negligible adsorption under pH = 8.5. This was because the negatively charged AMX in alkalinity caused serious electrostatic repulsion with the catalysts. This could inhibit the photocatalytic reactions to some extent. Similarly, low amounts of anionic LEV were adsorbed onto the negatively charged surface under pH = 8.5 (Guo et al., 2010). Moreover, the steady-state concentration of reactive species (•OH, H2O2 and •O2 −) under different solution pH was investigated. The optimal pH for the photocatalytic production of H2O2 was obtained at pH = 7 and pH = 8.5 while the amount of H2O2 declined under acid condition (Hou and Wang, 2017; Sharma et al., 2016; Song et al., 2019; Xue et al., 2022), which was discussed in detail in section 3.3.1. Due to the highest amount of H2O2 obtained under neutral pH, the concentration of •OH was the highest under pH = 7. A considerable amount of H2O2 was also obtained at pH = 8.5, but negligible amount of •OH was observed under the same condition due to the precipitation of iron ions. The decline of •OH concentration with pH in acid solution is in line with the change of degradation performance with pH. By contrast, the concentration of •O2 − exhibited little change in the whole range of pH. This indicates the higher catalytic efficiency was due to the higher ratios of •OH obtained under neutral pH, which was controversial to the homogeneous Fenton in which process low pH (2–4) favored the generation of •OH. It should be noted that although the solution pH was neutral, Fe2+(1 mg/L) mainly existed as dissolved ions according to the Ksp (4.87 × 10−17) of Fe(OH)2 (Hove et al., 2008). Additionally, Xing et al. have demonstrated that the unsaturated S of sulfides could capture H+ and provide an acidic microenvironment for Fenton reactions at neutral buffer solution (∼6.86) (Yan et al., 2021), thus promoting the activation of H2O2 under neutral pH. Consequently, the optimal condition for the production of •OH and degradation of antibiotics in this study was near-neutral pH (5–7).

Based on these results, the degradation mechanism of photocatalysis-self-Fenton was revealed. The decoration of ZnIn2S4 with NCDs increases light absorption, and photogenerated electrons could transfer to the surface of NCDs to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The enhanced visible light absorption and charge separation ability promote the reduction of oxygen molecules and the production of H2O2. In the photocatalytic process, active species mainly include h+, H2O2, and •O2 −, which react slowly with antibiotics. When the inner-generate H2O2 is transformed into •OH using a low concentration of Fe2+ under neutral solution pH, the decomposition of antibiotics accelerates significantly (Fig. 7d). Moreover, the low concentration of Fe2+ and no need to adjust solution pH in the proposed photocatalysis-Fenton system provide new thoughts for Fenton reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application potential of Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of salts, dissolved organic matters (DOM) and water quality on the photocatalytic performance were investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 , the degradation rate of LEV changed little with the presence of NaCl and Na2SO4 in contrast with slight decline with MgCl2 and CaCl2, indicating the inhibition effects of Mg2+ and Ca2+. Additionally, the removal rate of LEV decreased slightly to 88.3% with 50 mg/L HA. But in general, the degradation rate of LEV still showed over 85% removal rate with the presence of different substances. Moreover, the proposed self-fenton system showed nearly the same removal rate of LEV using deionized water, lake water and tap water, indicating the excellent adaptability to different water matrix (Guo et al., 2023). Apart from coexisting ions and DOM, the reusability of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 in the self-fenton process was explored. After each cycle, photocatalysts were filtrated, washed by deionized water and methanol three times, and finally dried for reuse. The photocatalysts were reused for five times and Fe2+ was added each cycle. The photocatalytic efficiency for each cycle was presented in Fig. S15. The composites exhibited high photocatalytic degradation rate (>90%) towards LEV in the five cycles in spite of slight decrease of degradation rate. Besides, there was no obvious change in the crystalline structure (Fig. S16), indicating the excellent structural stability of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites.

As mentioned above, photocatalysis-self-Fenton systems exhibited high efficiency towards LEV with the presence of different substances. To explore the efficiency under practical application, the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction utilizing NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and Fe2+ (1 mg/L) was conducted under real sunlight (sunny Sunday, 2:00 p.m., 30 °C, Guangzhou). As shown in Fig. 8c, the concentration of H2O2 reached 4.72 mg/L, which was higher than that in laboratory-simulated visible light. The concentration of •OH was 80.56 μM after 30 min, almost double that of laboratory-simulated visible light. The higher transformation efficiency of H2O2→•OH under natural solar light was due to the enhanced H2O2 production and higher decomposition rate of H2O2 under solar light irradiation. Due to the higher amounts of H2O2 and •OH, LEV was degraded efficiently with a removal rate of as high as 90.7% in 30 min. These results verified the high efficiency of the Fe2+/NCDs@ZnIn2S4-based system.

Additionally, the materials consumption of traditional Fenton and the proposed self-fenton system for the degradation of LEV were compared and the results were shown in Table S4. The data of the conventional Fenton was from Epold's reports (Epold et al., 2015). 75 μM LEV (about 27 mg/L LEV) was decomposed completely in 30 min via Fenton oxidation, similar to the degradation performance in this work. As shown in Table S4, large amounts of H2O2, iron salts and pH modifiers are required in traditional homogeneous Fenton. By contrast, only a small amount of iron salts is needed in the self-fenton systems. Although photocatalysts are required in the self-fenton system for the in situ production of H2O2, photocatalysts can be reused. Additionally, the costs of traditional Fenton mainly include material consumption and charges for sludge treatment. The costs of the proposed self-fenton are mainly composed of material consumption. Due to the negligible amount of iron salts required, the main costs stem from purchasing photocatalysts of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites. However, there are still no commercial NCDs@ZnIn2S4 or ZnIn2S4 composites available and the yield of catalysts prepared in the laboratory is low. Therefore, the mass production of catalyst materials is needed to be solved for the pilot-scale application of photo-self-fenton systems in the future.

The work successfully synthesized NCDs@ZnIn2S4 composites by hydrothermal method. TEM and XPS demonstrated the insertion of NCDs into ZnIn2S4 composites, and photoelectric analysis indicated that NCDs significantly promoted light absorption and charge transfer ability. The composites showed excellent H2O2 production via direct 2 e-ORR under visible light due to the excellent photoelectric performance. The concentration of H2O2 generated after 60 min light irradiation reached 152.8 μM. Moreover, the combination of NCDs@ZnIn2S4 and low concentration of Fe2+(1 mg/L) could remove over 81% LEV, AMX, and OTC. And the highest degradation efficiency occurred at neutral pH, which was attributed to the favorable adsorption and the highest •OH ratios under neutral pH. Besides, the proposed photocatalysis-self-Fenton exhibited high degradation efficiency towards LEV under real sunlight. The work shows the prospects of self-fenton systems in overcoming pH limitation and iron sludge problems in the Fenton process, but problems including the large-scale production of powder catalyst materials and the supply of adequate light irradiation need to be solved to expand its application in the future.

Xiaoman Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yuanhua Liu: Software, Investigation. Haiming Huang: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jianhua Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
